title-group.article-title: Gradient scaffolds in bone-soft tissue interface engineering: Structural characteristics, fabrication techniques, and emerging trends

contrib-group.aff: aShanghai Frontiers Science Center of Advanced Textiles, College of Textiles, Donghua University, Shanghai, 201620, China

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

sec.p: Bone and soft tissues are connected by a complex interface that is crucial for the smooth transfer of mechanical stress. Effective repair of this interface requires bio-scaffolds specifically designed to support the regeneration of diverse cell types and signalling molecules. With advances in micro- and nanotechnologies, gradient biomaterial scaffolds have demonstrated significant potential in interface tissue regeneration. This paper reviews the structure of the bone-soft tissue interface, the various gradient scaffold types, and construction methods. It also discusses the recent developments and future directions in interface tissue engineering, emphasizing the potential of gradient scaffolds to restore the natural structure and function of bone-soft tissue interfaces. Overall, this paper provides valuable insights into the application of gradient scaffolds for bone-soft tissue interface engineering, offering inspiration for biomimetic approaches to soft-hard interface repair in future medical engineering.

sec.title: The Translational Potential of this Article
sec.p: First, the emphasis on gradient scaffolds could significantly impact clinical practices related to bone-soft tissue integration, ultimately improving patient outcomes and quality of life. Second, it also aligns with the growing trend of biomimetic approaches in medical engineering, potentially inspiring new innovations in bone-soft tissue repair strategies.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: The interface between bone and soft tissues is prone to injury, and even after surgical intervention, it is challenging to achieve complete healing [1]. The primary reason lies in the replacement of crucial post-surgical healing tissue with disorganized fibrotic scar tissue, resulting in stress concentration and inefficient pressure transfer [2]. Complete attachment points transmit tensile, compressive, and shear stresses between soft tissues and bone through their intricate heterogeneous structures, effectively dissipating stress concentrations [3]. Common bone-soft tissue interfaces include bone-cartilage, bone-tendon, and bone-ligament [4], with attachment points characterized by fibrocartilaginous zones between the bone and tendon/ligament (T/L) [5]. Injuries to these interfaces are prevalent, with over 170,000 individuals in the United States experiencing some form of rotator cuff tendon tear annually. To prevent irreversible shoulder joint damage, over 10,000 rotator cuff tendon surgeries are performed worldwide each year, however, the re-tear rate remains high, ranging from 15 % to 94 % [6]. It is reported that over 300,000 shoulder, foot, and ankle surgeries, as well as more than 350,000 anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) surgeries, are performed annually in the U.S., with associated medical costs exceeding 30 billion dollars [7]. Following surgical reconstruction, inadequate tendon-bone healing of the ACL can result in various secondary symptoms, including knee joint laxity, movement instability, meniscal and cartilage injuries, and even early-onset post-traumatic osteoarthritis [8]. Given the complexity of reconstruction surgeries, there is an urgent need to improve traditional regenerative therapies.

Interface tissue engineering is regarded as a promising approach for regenerating interface tissues, addressing the limitations of allografts and autografts. An ideal tissue engineering scaffold should provide not only physical support for cell attachment and proliferation but also microstructural cues that mimic the natural extracellular matrix (ECM), guiding cells toward directed growth and differentiation [9]. Therefore, the goal of interface tissue engineering in this field is to replicate the structure-function relationships of natural tissue interfaces, enabling tissue scaffolds to perform similar physiological functions [10]. The gradient interface between bone and soft tissues is highly heterogeneous and anisotropic, with gradual changes in material composition from skeletal structure to soft tissue. Thus, the designed bone-soft tissue scaffolds should strive to closely mimic the structure and composition of natural tissue interfaces [11].

In this unique transitional zone between the bone and soft tissue interface, there are gradient changes in cell phenotype, matrix composition, tissue structure, and mechanical properties. To better mimic the complex hierarchical structure of natural interfaces, graft design has significantly improved, evolving from simple single-phase scaffolds to biphasic, triphasic, multiphasic, and, most recently, gradient scaffolds. Gradient biomimetic scaffolds used for interface repair demonstrate remarkable potential to mimic the intricate micro- and nanoscale tissue transitions in natural tissues [12]. This is primarily attributed to the biomimetic, progressive, and continuous transitions in composition and mechanical properties exhibited by gradient scaffold designs [[13], [14], [15]]. Compared to other reconstruction methods, which often struggle to address the stiffness differences between various tissues, gradient scaffolds gradually transition the mechanical properties of materials, effectively alleviating stress concentrations. This enhances the overall function and long-term stability of the interface. Additionally, gradient scaffolds, with their gradual biological, physical, and chemical variations, promote the integration of diverse cell types and tissue regeneration. Unlike traditional single-material or homogeneous scaffolds, gradient scaffolds better support specific cellular behaviours in distinct regions, such as cartilage formation, initiation of calcification, and bone generation. This is largely due to the inclusion of cells, signalling factors, and physical cues within the scaffold, which enhance ECM deposition and recovery by influencing interactions between adjacent natural tissue cells and the artificial scaffold [16]. In summary, gradient scaffolds, by closely mimicking the natural transitions in native tissues, represent a significant advancement in the engineering of bone-soft tissue interface repair [17]. Therefore, in the context of interface tissue engineering, thoroughly exploring the combination of these methods to simultaneously generate compositional, chemical, and cellular gradients as a means to enhance integration is highly promising.

In this review, we first summarize the three typical structures of bone-soft tissue interfaces and their gradient characteristics in terms of composition. Next, we introduce several widely recognized techniques in gradient scaffold manufacturing, emphasizing the strengths and notable achievements of different fabrication techniques. Furthermore, we summarize the fabrication process and contributions of gradient scaffolds by applying the aforementioned technologies to integrate various biochemical and physical cues. Additionally, we provide a brief review of advancements in tissue engineering, focusing on the most effective strategies for loading cells and growth factors (GFs) for interface tissue repair. The methods for incorporating cell and GF therapies into gradient scaffolds are also discussed. Finally, we discuss emerging trends and future directions in the rapidly advancing field of bone-soft tissue interfaces engineering.

sec.title: Bone-soft tissue interface structure
sec.p:
The musculoskeletal system endows the body with the ability to move through the attachment of muscles and bones. The main components of the system include connective tissue, such as bones, tendons, ligaments, and cartilage. Bone tissue and cartilage tissue respectively constitute the structural framework of bone and cartilage, belonging to solid connective tissues, while T/L are classified as fibrous connective tissues. The bone-soft tissue interfaces can be categorized into (1) bone-cartilage, (2) bone-tendon, and (3) bone-ligament interfaces, representing the transitions from hard to soft tissues [18]. For instance, the bone-ligament and bone-tendon interfaces, as typical examples of soft tissue to bone connections, exhibit similar interface features, as depicted in Fig. 1A、B. This interface consists of varying cells and matrix compositions across distinct spatial zones, resulting in three different tissue regions: T/L, fibrocartilage, and bone [19]. The fibrocartilage interface is further divided into mineralized and non-mineralized fibrocartilage zones, where the tendon/ligament directly attaches to the non-mineralized region of fibrocartilage, while the mineralized region attaches directly to the bone. This distribution helps dissipate interface stress concentration, generating a biomechanical gradient [20,21].Fig. 1
Structural composition of the bone-soft tissue interfaces. (A) bone-tendon interface [29,38]. Copyright 2019, Elsevier, and Copyright 2021, John Wiley and Sons; (B) bone-ligament interface [21,39]. Copyright 2019, John Wiley and Sons; (C) bone-cartilage interface [35,40]. Copyright 2020, American Chemical Society, and Copyright 2009, Elsevier.Fig. 1

Typically, the T/L region is characterized by oriented collagen fibres and elongated fibroblasts [22]. In the non-mineralized fibrocartilage region, these collagen fibres may be less prominent, exhibiting a higher proteoglycan content compared to T/L, and showing a transition from Type I to Type II collagen. Fibroblasts are replaced by fibrocartilage cells, which are rounder and situated in the interstices with a small amount of surrounding amorphous ECM [23]. In summary, the cartilage matrix consists of an amorphous matrix primarily composed of proteoglycans and water and collagen fibres embedded in it. In addition to collagen I and II, glycosaminoglycans (GAG) also exist [24]. The bone matrix comprises approximately 65 % inorganic components, mainly in the form of hydroxyapatite crystals, and 35 % organic components, primarily collagen I fibres, forming a mineralized collagen I matrix [25]. The characteristic gradient transition from bone to mineralized fibrocartilage is marked by fewer collagen fibres and mineral deposits, along with a sparse arrangement of fibrocartilage cells. This gradient transition characteristic positions the fibrocartilage region as a critical transitional zone between soft and hard tissues. It facilitates the gradual transfer of mechanical loads, reducing stress concentration, and thereby plays a crucial role in the overall functionality and durability of the bone-tendon/ligament interface [26]. Although tendons and ligaments share similarities, the design of the scaffold must also accommodate their respective specific loading environments. While both types of connective tissues can withstand high tensile loads, tendons typically endure uniaxial loading, whereas ligaments bear more torsional and tensile loads [27]. Therefore, when designing tendon or ligament-bone scaffolds, attention should be paid to distinguishing the different stress environments of the two. Replicating this transitional region is likely essential for ensuring proper tendon and ligament function in the context of tissue regeneration and repair [28,29].

The bone-cartilage interface consists of two distinct tissues, including subchondral bone and articular cartilage. Articular cartilage serves to protect subchondral bone, dissipate loads, and reduce joint friction, articular cartilage is composed of calcified cartilage and hyaline cartilage [30]. Articular cartilage is highly organized and consists of four distinct zones: the superficial zone (tangential), the middle zone (transitional), the deep zone (radial), and the calcified cartilage (Fig. 1C). Chondrocytes, ECM, and collagen fibres within the articular cartilage are anisotropic, especially in the superficial zone, where collagen fibres exhibit a preferential orientation. This anisotropy presents a significant challenge in the regeneration of the bone-cartilage interface [31]. In the superficial zone, chondrocytes are flattened, and collagen fibres are aligned parallel to the articular surface. In the transitional zone, collagen fibres gradually become oblique, with multiple chondrocytes arranged in columns. In the deep zone, collagen fibres are oriented perpendicular to the articular surface, and chondrocytes are clustered [32]. Within the calcified cartilage layer, hypertrophic chondrocytes are present, and the ECM appears calcified, containing additional collagen type X and minerals [33]. Cartilage tissue is primarily composed of a dense ECM, consisting of water, type II collagen, hypertrophic chondrocytes, and proteoglycans, with chondrocytes being the only cell type present in the cartilage [27]. The calcified cartilage and the subchondral bone plate interlock, forming a wavy boundary [32]. The subchondral bone plate and the underlying trabecular bone represent the dense layer of cortical bone and the spongy bone, respectively [34]. Bone tissue consists of a highly vascularized porous scaffold made of organic collagen mineralized with inorganic hydroxyapatite (HAp) [35]. Approximately 60%–70 % of bone mass is mineral, 25%–30 % is organic material, and 5%–10 % is water [34].

Natural osteochondral tissue exhibits a complex gradient heterogeneity, and many of the functional properties of joints derive from the unique gradient structure of the osteochondral unit [36]. The gradual gradient transition from cartilage to bone manifests as a progressive increase in biomechanical compression and elastic modulus, while the viscous modulus decreases. Additionally, the content of HAp and type I collagen (Col I) increases, whereas the levels of water and type II collagen (Col II) decrease. Structurally, the permeability and porosity of the vascular network gradually increase [31]. Therefore, tissue engineering scaffolds designed for osteochondral interfaces (OCI) should adequately consider tissues with distinct biochemical and mechanical properties. The goal is to cultivate chondrocyte differentiation using artificial scaffolds that possess both biochemical and biomechanical characteristics to generate new cartilage tissue [37]. It is essential to employ interface tissue engineering scaffolds that exhibit a gradual transition between bone and cartilage characteristics [34].

In the three interface tissue transitions, the mineralized gradient, as well as variations in cell types, matrix composition, and collagen fibre arrangement, all play a role in influencing the mechanical properties of the transition from soft tissue to hard tissue [23,41]. One emerging strategy for mimicking interface tissues is the development of gradient scaffolds. The use of gradient scaffolds aids in spatially guiding cell differentiation, facilitating the repair of damaged attachment points (e.g., through a cell shape gradient from fibroblasts to fibrocartilage cells and then to osteoblasts). Potential directions include designing scaffolds with gradients in stiffness and mineral content between soft tissue and bone, incorporating oriented fibres, and spatial deposition of collagen molecules for ECM formation or original fibre cross-linking [42]. In this approach, gradual changes in material, chemical, or physical components are more thoughtfully designed to summarize natural tissue transformation, which can lead to different expressions of cultured cells and have multiple positive effects [43]. While scaffolds for bone-soft tissue interface repair require both physical and biochemical gradients, the bone-T/L interface places greater emphasis on transitioning mechanical properties and simulating fibrous structures [44], whereas the bone-cartilage interface focuses more on scaffold microstructure and porosity to support cartilage matrix formation [45]. In contrast to other interfaces, such as the nerve–muscle interface, scaffolds must provide oriented topographical cues to guide cell or tissue growth direction. However, there are significant differences in material selection and bioactive factors. The nerve–muscle interface demands conductive materials and neurotrophic factors, while the bone-soft tissue interface priorities mechanical strength and growth factors for cartilage and bone formation [[46], [47], [48]]. Understanding the design and application of gradient scaffolds in other interface engineering contexts can help inform successful strategies to optimize scaffold design and improve repair outcomes.

sec.title: Gradient scaffold fabrication methods
sec.p: When designing gradient biomimetic scaffolds for bone-soft tissue interfaces regeneration, the primary consideration is to create an optimal microenvironment to guide both heterotypic and homotypic cell–cell communication, as well as facilitate cell–matrix interactions. Moreover, the scaffold must support cell growth and differentiation, facilitating the formation of graded tissues at the interface [49]. Scaffolds designed for interface tissue regeneration must present gradient features in composition and structure to mimic the native interface region. Advances in microscale and nanoscale technologies have facilitated the development of tissue scaffolds with gradient material properties and characteristics, allowing spatial control over cell differentiation and subsequent tissue formation.

To integrate gradient features into interface tissue engineering scaffolds, various technologies have been developed for manufacturing gradient scaffolds, including microfluidics, lithography, molecular migration (free diffusion, magnetic/electric drive), electrospinning, 3D printing, temperature control (Fig. 2). Here, we summarize the advantages and limitations of these fabrication techniques (Table 1).Fig. 2
Various gradient scaffold fabrication techniques, (A) Microfluidic technology; (B) Photolithography technology, including manufacturing using sliding masks and gradient masks; (C) Molecular migration; (D) Electrospinning; (E) 3D printing; (F) Temperature control.Fig. 2Table 1
Comparison of fabrication methods for different gradient scaffolds.Table 1

sec.title: Microfluidic technology
sec.p:
Microfluidic gradient generators commonly consist of two chambers, each pre-filled with high and low concentration solutions. Gradient generators have been widely used to create linear gradients of adhesive peptides (Fig. 3A) [79], GF (Fig. 3B) [80,81], and linear stiffness gradients of precursor hydrogel solutions [82]. Du et al. [50] utilized convection and alternating flow in microchannels to rapidly generate centimetre-scale gradients of biomolecules, polymers, microbeads, and cells, as well as intersecting gradients of two materials. Gradient concentrations of microbeads and cells were produced by the high-speed flow induced by high fluid shear provided by the microfluidic channel.Fig. 3
Gradient scaffolds fabricated using microfluidic technology. (A) polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) gradient-generating microfluidic device to immobilize the gradient of cellular adhesive Arg-Gly-Asp (RGD) peptide on polyethylene glycol (PEG) hydrogel [79], Copyright 2012, American Institute of Physics; (B) Using a microfluidic chip on which a tree flow pattern is implemented to create concentration gradients in a dichotomous manner [81], Copyright 2020, Royal Society of Chemistry.Fig. 3

In order to manufacture gradient scaffolds, microfluidic manufacturing devices often use stepwise multi-stage dilution and diffusion mixing [53]. Flow-based microfluidic platforms have been used to create biochemical signal gradients within biomaterial scaffolds. Microfluidics, as a practical experimental tool, can generate stable concentration gradients that are controlled in both spatial and temporal dimensions [83]. Various microfluidic platforms are used for the gradient formation [84], classified based on gradient generation principles into four main categories: (1) diffusion-based, (2) convection mixing-based, (3) laminar flow-based, and (4) geometrically defined mixer-based gradient generators [53]. Among them, the "tree-like gradient generator" based on convection mixing is the most notable. This generator consists of a microchannel network, where two monomer solutions are injected, and the microchannel network repeatedly splits and mixes the injected solutions, with the final mixed solution concentration detected at the outlet [85]. Microfluidic devices mostly exhibit microscale characteristics, and in order to broaden their application, He et al. [86] developed a method for creating centimetre-scale gradient hydrogels. This method used a pre-filled PDMS moulded microchannel, with one solution (typically a hydrogel precursor) introduced at the outlet and another solution with a high concentration of the target solute (also a hydrogel precursor) introduced at the inlet. Passive pumping induced forward flow of the inlet solution in the channel, and reflux, caused by evaporation at the channel inlet, led to the gradient of the target chemical substance, which was then stabilized by photopolymerization. In the application of interface tissue engineering, Lyu et al. [81] successfully utilized a microfluidic chip to generate a concentration gradient of osteogenic induction medium within the chambers of a decellularized tendon scaffold pre-seeded with bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells (BMSC) and tendon stem cells (TSC) (Fig. 3B). This approach effectively induced stem cells to respond to the concentration gradient, resulting in the differentiation of different cell types in various regions of the scaffold. Consequently, the extracellular matrix (ECM) composition and structure were remodelled, leading to the formation of distinct bone, tendon, and chondrocyte transition zones within the scaffold. similar research [87] suggests that microfluidic devices have been successfully used to produce nearly linear gradients within 3D hydrogel scaffolds. Therefore, microfluidic technology is considered a potential transformative approach in tissue engineering. However, the gradients generated by this method are predominantly at the microscale, and the mechanical properties still require optimization.

sec.title: Mask-based photolithography technology
sec.p:
For photoactive hydrogels, various gradient photomasks (radial, linear, and exponential) can be employed to create gradients. In this technique, hydrogel precursors are first placed in a mold, and a mask is applied between the hydrogel and a ultraviolet (UV) light source during the polymerization process [84]. The gradient can be generated by sliding the photomask. Marklein and Burdick manufactured a gradient methacrylate hyaluronic acid hydrogel by using a photomask to move at a constant line speed of 10 mm/min between the light source and the gel surface (Fig. 4A) [88]. Further patterning of stiffness gradient hydrogels can be achieved through photomasks. Stephanie et al. utilized photomask tools to manufacture stiffness gradient hydrogels using polyethylene glycol diacrylate (PEGDA) hydrogels with varying polymer chain lengths [89]. The 20 kDa PEGDA base hydrogel was crosslinked under UV light for 45 s. Subsequently, the base hydrogel was immersed overnight in a solution containing 3.4 kDa PEGDA and a photo-initiator. After a simple rinse, the gel was subjected to UV light for 1 min through a photomask to restrict crosslinking to the patterned area. By altering the pattern percentage, the hydrogel modulus ranged from 9.9 kPa to 167.1 kPa.Fig. 4
Methods of gradient scaffolds fabricated by photolithography technology. (A) By confining ultraviolet light to certain regions of the additive cross-linked gel, patterns are created using a photomask (left) or by altering the UV exposure time (through a sliding mask) to create gradients (right) [88]. Copyright 2010, Royal Society of Chemistry; (B) Gradient patterns are generated and exposed on the sample surface [54]. (a) A grayscale photomask is used, (b) stiffness gradients are produced on the hydrogel surface, scale bar = 250 μm, (c) Young's modulus of 20 and 10 wt % gels along the gradient position. Copyright 2016, American Chemical Society; (C) Schematic of patterned hydrogel photolithography on a silicon wafer coated with a sacrificial layer [90] (a), casting a solution of copolymer film (thickness h = 7–17 mm), and exposing it to low-dose ultraviolet light through the first photomask (b), followed by high-dose ultraviolet light exposure through the second photomask (c), After removing unreacted polymer (d), the patterned hydrogel is immersed in a water solution, dissolving the bottom sacrificial layer and releasing it from the substrate (e). The dimensions of the pattern are as shown in (f). Copyright 2019, Wiley-VCH.Fig. 4

Another approach is direct gradient photolithography, where gradients can be created by non-contact patterning of a light-degradable hydrogel (Fig. 4B) [54]. Photolithography is a powerful technique for controlling the spatial distribution of components or cross-linking density within a gel matrix. In this context, microscale grayscale images projected by microscopy can create specific patterns in hydrogels, including stiffness gradients. The cross-linking density of photocrosslinkable polymers and the corresponding swelling ratio of hydrogels can be controlled by the grayscale value and exposure time of the photomask. By using grayscale photolithography masks, a smooth contour of cross-linking density can be encoded within hydrogels, subsequently encoding a swelling ratio gradient (Fig. 4C) [90]. Specifically, this method utilizes a digital micromirror array device (DMD) to control the dosage of UV light, thereby regulating the degree of swelling in the shape-programmable, photocrosslinkable poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAm) copolymer films [91]. However, whether using sliding photomask or direct gradient lithography, the gradient support formed is limited to shallow gradients.

sec.title: Molecular migration
sec.p: Free diffusion leading to molecular migration between two levels is a simple method of creating gradients, which typically utilizes two solutions of the same chemical substance with different concentrations or two solutions with different compositions. These solutions are typically deposited at either end of a mold or an already formed substrate, with the ports at both ends acting as donors and acceptors for the diffusion of compounds in space [92]. For instance, when a hydrogel is enriched with high-concentration molecules, these molecules diffuse within the hydrogel, creating molecular concentration gradients within the gel. This method has been employed to generate protein gradients [93]. Masaya et al. [94] introduced a diffusion-controlled fabrication techniques that simplifies the creation of functional group gradients within three-dimensional scaffolds. This gradient results from the hydrolysis diffusion of amine groups in a PAAm hydrogel, leading to the formation of a sodium hydroxide (NaOH) concentration gradient. This approach demonstrated that the initial NaOH concentration and reaction time for amine groups are critical for controlling the carboxylic acid concentration gradient. Lee et al. [95] reported diffusion-based solution precursor for generating stiffness gradients, primarily utilizing finite mixing in a Hele-Shaw device to create stiffness gradients in polyacrylamide hydrogels. Here, two precursors with different stiffness were mixed in connected holes (for up to 80 min), resulting in a stiffness gradient of 4.1 kPa/mm after polymerization. But this gradient based on molecular free diffusion is not stable during the formation process.

Utilizing magnetic control for directional migration of molecules is also beneficial for the formation of gradient scaffolds. Incorporating magnetic nanoparticles (MNP) into 3D cell carriers, such as hydrogels, can provide magnetic responsiveness, leading to functional structures with biochemical, physical, and mechanical properties that can be regulated in the cellular microenvironment [59]. Zhang et al. [60] embedded superparamagnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles into HA nanorods using a chemical precipitation method to form MagHA. Under the influence of a magnetic field, these nanoscale MagHA particles migrated vertically downward in the pre-hydrogel solution, creating a gradient of increasing concentration. This gradient was subsequently fixed once the hydrogel was crosslinked. This distribution of MagHA mimics the continuous low-to-high mineral, mechanical, and biophysical gradient observed in natural osteochondral tissue from the superficial zone to the subchondral bone region. Consequently, it provides an adaptable composite hydrogel system that promotes cell infiltration and specific differentiation.

Similarly, the application of an electric field can create gradient characteristics within microstructures. Using ionic liquids [64], HA nanoparticles [76], and charged biopolymer chains, local properties within gradient scaffolds can be flexibly designed. For instance, Miao et al. [63] utilized an electric field to separate a uniform bicomponent mixture of gelatine and chitosan, resulting in the production of Janus films with integrated compositional and microstructural gradients. Compositionally, the Janus films exhibit a transition from a chitosan-rich layer to a layer containing both chitosan and gelatine. Structurally, these Janus films transition from a dense, non-porous layer (rich in chitosan) to a loose, porous layer (containing both chitosan and gelatine). In summary, the utilization of electric and magnetic field effects to achieve specific gradient structural features is feasible and holds significant potential.

sec.title: Electrospinning technique
sec.p:
Nano fibres have found extensive application in tissue engineering materials such as bone scaffolds [96], meniscus repair materials, rings, and ligaments [97]. They exhibit high aspect ratios, high surface area-to-volume ratios, tuneable mechanical properties [98], and the required porosity for effective cell growth in an ECM-mimicking environment. Electrospinning is widely used to create nanofibre scaffolds that resemble collagen fibres in the ECM, electrospun scaffolds can be processed using a controlled deposition process to incorporate mineral gradients [99]. Furthermore, the manufacturing process can optimize fibre orientation to match the functional characteristics of the target tissue [100]. The electrospinning process can be easily modified to produce structural or compositional gradients within one setting (Fig. 5A) [101,102]. For example, altering the speed of the collector in an electrospinning process can impart an orientation gradient to the fibres [103]. Utilizing a dual-chamber electrospinning reservoir, where an interface port is established in a single reservoir to form two separate compartments, allows for controlled mixing of solutions through the port. This controlled mixing in the electrospinning reservoir can impart a compositional gradient to the jet [104]. Bidirectional gradient electrospinning can also easily create gradient scaffolds, providing material gradients to the collected fibres by varying the flow rates of different solutions [105].Fig. 5
Electrostatic spinning technique for fabrication of gradient scaffolds. (A) Modification of the electrospinning process to prepare gradient fibres [101], (a) imparting fibre orientation gradient by varying the collection electrode speed, (b) combining a microfluidic device with the electrospinning apparatus, (c) controlled mixing of solutions in the electrospinning reservoir, (d) dual electrospinning of different solutions with varying flow rates. Copyright 2017, John Wiley and Sons; (B) Electrospinning to create ultrafine fibre-reinforced nanofibre scaffolds with structural and material gradients [99], (a) electrospinning highly oriented Poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA) nanofibres, (b) incorporation of Polylactic Acid (PLA) microfibres, (c) electrospinning highly oriented PLGA nanofibres, (d) electrospinning random PLGA/nHA/BMP-2 nanofibres, followed by cutting and rolling to form 3D gradient scaffolds. Copyright 2017 Royal Society of Chemistry; (C) Building dual gradients through self-assembly on oriented electrospun nanofibre films [106], (a) schematic of constructing collagen (COL) and chondroitin sulphate (CS) gradient structures on uniaxially oriented polycaprolactone (PCL) nanofibres, (b) schematic of the layer-by-layer self-assembly process to prepare gradient scaffolds. Copyright 2022 Elsevier; (D) Double-layer random electrospun scaffolds with mineralization gradients [107]. Copyright 2017 Elsevier.Fig. 5

Electrospun nanofibres are also amenable to surface modification by combining them with bioactive agents to enhance cell compatibility [108]. For instance, by combining mineral deposition with collagen-arranged electrospun nanofibres, bone tissue can be simulated. This type of scaffold promotes stem cell attachment, differentiation, collagen synthesis, and mineralization [109]. He et al. [99]. developed PLGA scaffolds with 3D gradient ligaments enhanced by rolled microfibre using a unique electrospinning strategy that generated structure and composition gradients within PLGA scaffolds. By encapsulating nHA and bone morphogenetic protein-2 (BMP-2) in electrospun PLGA nanofibres, the orientation of the fibres and the concentrations of nHA and BMP gradually changed from the bone region to the ligament region (Fig. 5B). Li et al. [107]. designed a bilayered random electrospun mat containing mineral gradients by electrospinning poly (L-lactic acid) (PLLA) and nHap-PLLA nanofibres on a rotating drum collector, with the first layer consisting of pure PLLA and the second layer introducing nHap-PLLA nanofibres to improve tendon-to-bone repair (Fig. 5D).

Given that using a single electrospinning technique may be insufficient to fabricate interface tissue engineering scaffolds with multiple compositional and functional gradients, especially for customized scaffolds with specific clinical requirements, electrospinning is often combined with other techniques [110]. layer-by-layer (LBL) technology has already been combined with electrospinning to create scaffolds with uniform surface structures (Fig. 5C) [106]. Additionally, extrusion and electrospinning have been combined to create gradient nanofibre composite materials, a hybrid technology involving twin-screw extrusion [111]. Combining extrusion and electrospinning enables the incorporation of various components into electrospinning in a time-dependent manner, resulting in gradient nanofibre scaffolds with spatial gradation characteristics [112]. Additionally, combining electrospinning with microfluidics can endow nanofibre scaffolds with customized bioactivity [113]. For instance, microfluidic technology can be employed to produce monodisperse calcium alginate microbeads, which, when combined with electrospun nanofibres, result in a novel nano-micro alternated multilayer scaffold. These microbeads, loaded with BMSCs and the growth factor BMP-2, have shown enhanced osteogenic differentiation and ectopic bone formation in vivo [114]. In conclusion, whether using standalone electrospinning technology or integrating it with other fabrication techniques, it is possible to develop bio-scaffolds with structural and compositional gradients. This capability provides significant practical value in the repair of interface tissues.

sec.title: 3D printing
sec.p: A novel method for creating gradients is 3D bioprinting [84]. Using 3D printing for layer-by-layer biofabrication provides precise spatial control over the deposition of biological materials and cells. Bioprinters typically feature two or more printheads, enabling the generation of gradients during the printing process by combining various cells in hydrogels or precursor hydrogels at different densities [115,116]. The mixing process during 3D printing necessitates a complete system where the viscosity of bio-ink, extrusion flow rates, printing speed, cell concentrations, and reaction kinetics of molecules to be immobilized are compatible with the chosen 3D bioprinting technology [117]. In this process, the viscosity of the bioink must be sufficiently high to prevent the formation of tension-driven droplets at the nozzle tip, allowing for the extrusion of continuous filaments. The main advantage of 3D printing is precise spatial control, enabling the generation of multiple gradients within adjustable material scaffolds. However, there are few reports demonstrating that 3D printing can simultaneously meet the requirements of forming stiffness and cell density gradients as well as the immobilization of signalling molecules [118].

The most common 3D printing technologies are extrusion bioprinting, laser-assisted bioprinting (LAB), and inkjet bioprinting (Fig. 6A) [119]. Among these, only extrusion bioprinting and inkjet bioprinting can easily create a variety of compositional gradients. 3D printing utilizes multiple print heads to integrate various materials, including hydrogels, easily extrudable synthetic polymers, and ceramics. Additionally, 3D bioprinting facilitates the rapid and on-demand biomimetic design of complex soft and hard tissue interface structures with intricate architectures and chemical cues. For example, Sun et al. [120] developed a dual growth factor-releasing scaffold with gradient pore structures using 3D bioprinting. Results from animal models indicated that the gradient scaffolds could provide anisotropic cartilage formation in different layers and structural support for newly formed cartilage tissue. Furthermore, compared to the small-pore group, the gradient scaffolds significantly enhanced the ingrowth of micro vessels. Smith et al. [121] designed a 3D printed porous composite PCL-based scaffold with vertical gradients of porosity and pore size, as well as ceramic content gradients of β-TCP. Research has shown that areas with higher porosity exhibit enhanced osteogenic differentiation and mineralization, and the concentration of β - TCP is the main factor affecting the osteogenic differentiation of MSCs. Similarly, Bittner et al. [122] used a multi-material extrusion 3D printing system to fabricate scaffolds with dual gradients in porosity and HA content (Fig. 6B), mimicking the gradients found in natural osteochondral tissue. These findings collectively demonstrate the potential of 3D bioprinting in constructing scaffolds with compositional and structural gradients.Fig. 6
Gradient brackets fabricated by different 3D printing techniques. (A) These printing technologies include inkjet printing, laser bioprinting, and extrusion bioprinting [116,119]. Copyright 2015, Elsevier and Copyright 2018, Elsevier; (B) Dual gradient scaffolds with porosity and HA content prepared by a multi material extrusion 3D printing system [122]. Copyright 2018, WILEY-VCH; (C) Three regions with different surface morphologies were designed through indirect 3D printing, including a gradient of bone porosity, as well as cavities mimicking tendon and muscle fibres [123].Fig. 6

Emerging bioprinting strategies have been employed for the direct printing and spatial patterning of cells and chemical factors to simulate the complexity of tissues in vivo. Cai et al. [124] used 3D cell printing and tissue-specific decellularized ECM (dECM) bio-inks to create gradient multi-tissue interface scaffolds mimicking the tendon-bone interface (TBI). These interface scaffolds displayed gradient characteristics like natural TBIs in structure, composition, and cellular heterogeneity. They were functionally verified by real-time monitoring of interface tissue formation and regeneration, supported by a rat model. These 3D cell-printed structures have shown promise for gradient interface reconstruction and shoulder cuff function recovery. Balestri et al. [123] developed composite hydrogels based on collagen/agarose collagen with stiffness gradients achieved by adding varying concentrations of HA (Fig. 6C). A 3D in vitro model was manufactured through indirect 3D printing, featuring three sections with tissue-specific surface morphology and biologically relevant surface morphologies, mimicking tendon and muscle fibres, were designed. While 3D bioprinting is suitable for designing various types of gradient scaffolds, the resolution of commonly used 3D printers is typically limited to the microscale. Additionally, 3D printing equipment demands high precision, requiring specialized operation to achieve the desired outcomes.

sec.title: Temperature control
sec.p: Directional freezing and thawing (DFT) have been demonstrated as an inexpensive and convenient method for preparing gradient materials. By unidirectionally freezing the solvent, solvent crystals align along the freezing direction to form a porous structure, thus creating materials with aligned porous structures [76]. DFT typically utilizes polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) as the material of choice. PVA aqueous solutions can be converted into hydrogels through repeated freeze–thaw cycles, forming microcrystals without the need for any potentially toxic chemical crosslinking agents [125]. Kim et al. [126] employed a gradual freezing method using liquid nitrogen (LN2) to prepare cylindrical PVA hydrogels with a hardness gradient. The prepared cylindrical PVA hydrogels exhibited a progressively increasing hardness along the longitudinal axis, ranging from approximately 1 kPa at the top to around 24 kPa at the bottom (the side in contact with LN2). Furthermore, Oh et al. [77] incorporated hyaluronic acid (HA) to enhance the cell adhesion properties of PVA hydrogels. Using DFT, they prepared cylindrical PVA/HA hydrogels with a wide range of stiffness gradients (from ∼20 kPa to ∼200 kPa) and enhanced cell adhesion. In vitro cell culture experiments using these stiffness-gradient PVA/HA hydrogels demonstrated that human bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells (hMSCs) exhibited stiffness-dependent differentiation into specific cell types. The different stiffness ranges induced differentiation into various cell types: approximately 20 kPa for neurons, 40 kPa for muscle cells, 80 kPa for chondrocytes, and 190 kPa for osteoblasts. These findings highlight the significant potential of DFT-prepared gradient hydrogels in interfacial tissue engineering applications.

Due to the specific cross-linking mechanisms typically associated with DFT, its applicability is limited and may not accommodate the growing diversity of new hydrogel systems. Alexandros et al. [75] addressed this limitation by combining microfluidic technology with the physical phenomenon of thermophoresis to fabricate stiffness-gradient hydrogels. This thermophoretic approach offers a non-toxic and efficient method that can be applied to various types of hydrogels, not confined to a single cross-linking mechanism and extending beyond photopolymerizable biomaterials. This demonstrates that temperature control, coupled with microfluidic technology, can provide innovative strategies for the construction of gradient scaffolds.

sec.title: Scaffolds with different gradient cues
sec.p: To restore the functionality of tissue interfaces, scaffolds must meet several key criteria. They must possess suitable biocompatibility and biodegradability, degrading in a controlled manner to allow for cell ingrowth and new ECM formation. The porosity of the scaffolds must permit cell infiltration, proliferation, nutrient exchange, and waste removal. The mechanical properties must match the structural requirements of both soft and hard tissues. The morphology of the scaffolds should display varying fibre orientations and mimic natural ECM characteristics. Additionally, they should replicate the natural mineral gradient from fibrocartilage to bone tissue, ensuring proper mineralization [7].

Current research efforts primarily focus on mechanical gradients, including topological structure, stiffness, and material porosity, as well as biochemical gradients. This focus aims to ensure effective transmission of stress across complex tissue interfaces, promoting cell diffusion, differentiation, and migration. The preparation methods for scaffolds with different gradient cues and their roles in interface tissue regeneration are summarized in Table 2. From a material development standpoint, nanofibrous scaffolds that mimic the morphology and structure of natural ECM have attracted much attention in tissue engineering due to their capacity to serve as a matrix for cell adhesion, proliferation, differentiation, and ECM formation [127]. Similarly, hydrogels that exhibit ECM-like characteristics possess viscoelasticity and diffusion transport properties [128]. Their chemical and physical properties are tuneable, making them ideal for creating and customizing 3D cell microenvironments. Hydrogel matrices composed of tissue-relevant biomaterials, such as gelatine, alginate, hyaluronic acid, and chitosan, mimic the chemical environment of the ECM and provide essential adhesion sites for cells [129].Table 2

sec.title: Biochemical gradient
sec.p: Biochemical gradient is the concentration gradient of bioactive molecules, Biochemical signals play a pivotal role in guiding cellular behaviour both in vivo and in vitro. In the context of repairing bone-to-soft tissue interfaces, nearly all pathological and physiological processes entail the establishment of gradients, including biochemical gradients. These processes can be reconstructed in vitro using biomaterial scaffolds that incorporate biochemical gradients. Additionally, achieving complex gradient tissues such as bone-cartilage tissue in vitro requires the slow spatial release of GF. Specific components of T/L, cartilage, calcified cartilage, and bone layers also need to be taken into account [138]. The concentration signals of different GF, influencing cell behaviour, will be discussed in detail in the following section.

In addition to GF, inorganic ions have been shown to be crucial for the normal synthetic metabolic functions of tissues and tissue-specific cell functions [139]. Biomimetic minerals, structurally similar to natural bone minerals, with mineral-based biomaterials possessing bone-conductive and potential bone-inductive properties, have been demonstrated to promote osteogenesis [127]. For instance, calcium phosphate cement (CPC), as a promising bone substitute, can serve as a bone-mimicking component in soft material modifications. Introducing CPC components into inert ligament materials, effectively connecting with host soft tissues, can regenerate an ideal soft-hard interface by enhancing CPC-induced bone integration [140]. In vitro depositing calcium and phosphate onto tendon tissue, achieved through a gradient mineralization induced by embryonic protein treatment, may contribute to the creation of a natural transition zone within the tendon. Mineral deposition is visible at a depth of only 200 μm [141]. Similarly, Chen et al. [127] gradient-mineralized SF nanofibre scaffolds, with a resulting high concentration mineralization zone having a Ca/P ratio of 1.64, closely resembling biological hydroxyapatite. The results suggest that this gradient scaffold can enhance the integration of the tendon-bone interface, leading to higher ultimate loads and increased formation of fibrocartilage-like tissue. Another study [133] altered the immersion time of PLGA and PCL nanofibre scaffolds in concentrated simulated body fluid, forming a linear gradient of calcium phosphate on the scaffold. This mineral gradient along the scaffold's length imparts a gradient in mechanical properties, where lower strain and higher elastic modulus correspond to regions with higher concentrations of calcium phosphate [142]. Additionally, collagen scaffolds with a composition gradient formed by a RUNX2 retrovirus coating can induce fibroblasts to generate a gradient of mineralized matrix both in vitro and in vivo [143]. Although the spatial resolution of these linear gradients has not yet precisely matched the physiological range [144], these gradient scaffold systems hold significant guidance for research on the regeneration of biomimetic tendon-bone interfaces [127].

Among the biochemical factors, oxygen tension plays a crucial role in cellular fate during skeletal development. Under physiological conditions, bone cells typically reside in relatively oxygen-rich environments, while chondrocytes exist in hypoxic environments [145]. Research indicates that oxygen plays a role in guiding the integration of cartilage and bone. While hypoxic conditions (2.5 % O2) is prone to suppress hypertrophy and endochondral ossification of MSCs initiated for chondrogenesis [146]. Although spatial variation in oxygen levels is challenging to achieve within gradient scaffolds using current technologies, this emerging avenue of controlling oxygen concentration may offer new insights into bone-soft tissue interface engineering.

sec.title: Stiffness gradient
sec.p:
Cells have the remarkable ability to actively sense the stiffness of their extracellular matrix, often displaying a preference for migrating towards stiffer surfaces. This phenomenon is commonly called as durotaxis [147]. Research indicates that cells exhibit higher speeds on softer matrices compared to stiffer counterparts [148]. Surface stiffness can regulate the adhesion, proliferation, and differentiation of stem cells. In fact, MSC tend to become more dispersed and differentiate into cartilaginous and osteogenic lineages on substrates with higher stiffness (ranging from 50 to 90 kPa), while softer surfaces (30–50 kPa) lead to tendon-like phenotypes [146]. Matrix with elastic moduli close to those of the brain, cartilage, muscle, and bone directly induces differentiation of stem cells, especially mesenchymal stem cells, into neurons, chondrocytes, myocytes, and osteocytes. Stem cells can perceive matrix stiffness and regulate the amount of actin filaments and focal adhesion components to generate deformation [149](Fig. 7A and B). Epithelial monolayer migration on stiffness gradients has been shown to be a result of mechanisation-induced polarization and directed migration, rather than random mobility on a single stiffness matrix [150]. Dynamic approaches have demonstrated that gradually stiffening collagen matrices over the length of a sample using strain gradients results in mammary epithelial cells moving from organ-like into areas of higher stiffness [151].Fig. 7
The influence of stiffness on cellular behaviour. (A) Different substrate stiffness promotes multipotent differentiation of stem cells [149]; (B) On a soft matrix, stem cells exhibit small spreading morphology, unclear actin cytoskeleton, and low levels of laminin-adhesion protein-A. Stem cells cultured on a stiff matrix display large spreading morphology, significant stress fibres, high levels of laminin-adhesion protein-A, and strong focal adhesion complexes [149]. (C) Stiffness gradient multi-domain hydrogel scaffold [136], (a) Electron microscopy images and cross-sectional microstructures of LMWGs, LMWG-PEGDA, and PEGDA gels; (b) Mechanical properties of LMWGs, LMWG-PEGDA, and PEGDA gels, including storage modulus (G′), loss modulus (G″), and (c) tanδ in the frequency sweep model. Copyright 2021, Elsevier.Fig. 7

In the early studies of material stiffness gradients, the widely used photolithography technique was predominantly employed for its ease of operation. For example, Raimon et al. [152] irradiated a solution containing acrylamide/bis-acrylamide, including irgacure, sandwiched between two 170 μm thick cover glasses. Initially, the sample was masked by an opaque cover, and the gel solution gradually received radiation gradients as the mask was moved at a controlled speed [102]. The results demonstrated the creation of linear gradient stiffness hydrogels, with a Young's modulus ranging from 1 to 240 kPa, yielding at least 115 kPa/mm. The exposure time and intensity of ultraviolet light are crucial factors in determining the hardness of crosslinked hydrogels. When fibroblasts were seeded on stiffness gradient hydrogels (ranging from 0.5 to 22 kPa), Nuria et al. [153] observed that fibre adhesion exhibited mechanical responsiveness. This responsiveness demonstrated a logarithmic and tensin-1 dependent increase with stiffness. The length of the fibres increased rapidly at lower stiffness levels (0.5–7 kPa) and gradually plateaued at higher stiffness levels (7–22 kPa).

Similarly, the gradual exposure of cell-loaded photodegradable polyethylene glycol hydrogels to ultraviolet light results in a hardness gradient on the scaffold, leading to gradient cell differentiation [154]. Zhang et al. [136] created a multi-domain gel scaffold for bone-cartilage defects by incorporating/unincorporating low-molecular-weight gels (LMWG) within a stable PEGDA network through photopolymerization. Selecting different materials to modify biological, topological, and mechanical properties, the stiffness gradient of the multi-domain gel ranges from approximately 20 kPa in the cartilage layer to around 300 kPa in the bone layer along the longitudinal direction, with spatial variations at the gradient interface (Fig. 7C). However, to date, the specific relationship and mechanisms by which hydrogel stiffness affects BMSC behaviour at the interface remain inconclusive. An intriguing circular patterned hydrogel model with a stiffness gradient has been employed to screen for the most favourable ECM environment for specific cells or applications [155]. This circular patterned hydrogel is composed of six segments with stiffness ranging from 1.2 to 28.9 kPa. Under the guidance of unevenly distributed stiffness stress, stem cells successfully migrated from the moderately stiff central region to the surrounding hydrogel with varying stiffness gradients, exhibiting differences in migration rate and distance. Studies have shown that BMSCs exposed to stiffness gradient hydrogels convert biophysical cues into biochemical signals through mechanical transduction processes, with the activated FAK-MAPK pathway playing a significant role in promoting osteogenic differentiation [156]. Regarding morphological markers, there is a clear positive correlation between cell aspect ratio and the stiffness of the underlying substrate. The expression of mechanical markers, such as Lamin A, YAP, and MRTFa, also shows a significant correlation with stiffness. This correlation can be disrupted by inhibiting non-muscle myosin or the Rho/ROCK signalling pathway [157].

sec.title: Porous structure gradient
sec.p: Structural microenvironment cues also play a pivotal role in guiding cellular behaviour both in vivo and in vitro. Porosity plays a crucial role in the diffusion of nutrients and oxygen, especially in the absence of a functional vascular system, and natural tissues often possess nanoscale pores [33]. The pore structure of hydrogels, including pore size, orientation, connectivity, and hierarchy, is influenced by the polymer chain structure, length, and cross-linker density. Previous reports have suggested that pore sizes ranging from 100 μm to 300 μm are ideal for bone regeneration. Larger pores facilitate inward vascular growth, while smaller pores around 100 μm promote hypoxia and encourage avascular or fibrous tissue regeneration [49]. Some studies have indicated that in vitro, lower porosity stimulates bone formation by inhibiting cell proliferation and promoting cell aggregation. In vivo, higher porosity and pore size lead to greater inward bone growth. Smaller pores are advantageous for hypoxic conditions and induce cartilage formation before osteogenesis, while well-vascularized large pores result in direct osteogenesis without prior cartilage formation [158].

Porous structure gradients can be created through various methods, including electrospinning, gradual freeze-drying, particle leaching, and 3D printing (Fig. 8B) [160]. Zhang et al. fabricated alginate scaffolds with various gradient pore structures through freeze-drying [161], By adjusting the initial temperature and duration, the scaffold exhibits a vertically oriented structure with honeycomb-shaped pores that gradually expand in size, ranging from 10 to 65 μm at the bottom to 50–141 μm at the top. demonstrating that different freezing methods under changing temperatures could produce progressively increasing pore sizes and variable pore shapes. Particle leaching refers to the process where the melt is first mixed and then subjected to particle leaching. The advantage of this method is the ease of controlling and predicting porosity (the ratio of pores to total volume) and pore size by controlling the number and size of leachable particles [162]. Smith et al. [121] used 3D printing to construct double-gradient scaffolds of pores and β-TCP, independently investigating the effects of pore gradient and ceramic composition gradient on cell phenotypes. The research revealed that during late-stage culture, larger pores showed greater potential for promoting osteoblast cell maturation compared to smaller pores. Luca et al. [137] used 3D printing to create scaffolds with multiscale pore gradients to investigate their effects on proliferation, differentiation, and ECM deposition of seeded MSC (Fig. 8C). The results indicated an incremental increase in cartilage formation markers and ECM deposition with decreasing pore sizes within the gradient scaffolds. Furthermore, it was found that modifying the fibre deposition pattern between two consecutive layers allowed to produce 3D scaffolds with a stepped pore structure gradient. 3D printing can also be combined with other technologies, such as grayscale light projection. Hari et al. [159] introduced grayscale digital light projection 3D printing technology to polymerize phase-separating inks, controlling the porosity of the material formed during ink polymerization induced phase separation. With increasing grayscale intensity, material crosslinking decreases, reducing the density of the porous network while increasing its porosity, thereby promoting the formation of functionally graded porous materials (Fig. 8A). Various types of porous gradient scaffolds for bone-tissue interface engineering are shown in Table 3. A single gradient of porous structures is typically applied to osteochondral interfaces, while providing mechanical and structural support. Considering the microscale and multi-tissue composition of the T/L-bone interface, a combined strategy incorporating both appropriate biochemical signals and mechanical gradients would be most beneficial for functional regeneration of the soft-hard tissue interface.Fig. 8
Pore gradient scaffolds in interfacial tissue engineering. (B) SEM images of a porous bone-cartilage scaffold [160]. (a) Cross-sectional view of the entire scaffold for each unique region. (b) Partially melted PCL fibres. (c) Cross-sectional image of the porogen-electrospinning interface. (d) Vertical channels through directional freeze foaming. sec.title: Fibro structure gradient
sec.p: The T/L-bone interface is characterized by unique biological cues. In tendon and ligament tissues, a well-organized extracellular matrix (ECM) is composed of perfectly aligned and densely packed fibres [172]. This fibre anisotropy guides cell alignment and promotes orderly tissue regeneration. One major cause of tendon-bone healing failure is the deposition of disorganized tissue near the tendon, rather than the formation of structured collagen fibres. Aligned nanofibres can enhance tendon-bone integration by guiding the formation of anisotropic tissues with matching mechanical properties [131]. Therefore, an oriented matrix capable of providing spatial cell arrangement may represent a promising strategy for tendon-bone interface tissue engineering.

Electrospinning technology is a relatively common and straightforward method for fabricating fibre-oriented structures [173]. Using electrospinning, it is possible to create scaffolds that include both highly oriented and randomly aligned nanofibres within the same scaffold [174], effectively mimicking the fibre orientation changes present at the tendon-to-bone insertion site. Tendon fibroblasts exhibit highly oriented morphology in the fibre-aligned regions and random morphology in the disordered regions. Cai et al. [175] fabricated a bilayer scaffold by first producing a random film through electrospinning and then increasing the rotation speed to obtain an overlapping oriented layer. In a rabbit model of autologous tendon-bone healing, this bilayer scaffold induced new bone formation, increased the fibrocartilage area, improved collagen organization and maturation, and effectively enhanced tendon-bone integration. Studies have shown that aligned nanofibres promote tenogenic differentiation of stem cells, while random nanofibres promote osteogenic differentiation [[176], [177], [178], [179]].

Recently, the integration of biochemical cues with physical cues from oriented nanofibres has provided new insights for soft-hard interface tissue healing. Biomimetic mineralization has shown positive effects in the fibrocartilage transition zone, representing the gradual transition from soft tissue (i.e., tendon or ligament) to hard tissue (i.e., mineralized bone) [180]. Using electrospinning or wet spinning techniques, scaffolds can be fabricated with mineralized random fibres at one end and aligned oriented fibres at the other. These fibrous scaffolds, with continuous topographical and compositional gradients, can mimic the characteristics of the soft-to-hard tissue interface, aiding in multi-tissue regeneration [174,[181], [182], [183]]. However, current research in this area lacks in vivo studies. Establishing and repairing soft-hard tissue injury models in vivo will be a crucial focus for future research.

sec.title: Tissue engineering strategies in the gradient scaffolds
sec.p: The complex structures and cellular compositions of natural bone-soft tissue interfaces make replicating their biological functions challenging. Interface tissue engineering focuses on regenerating these interfaces and offers potential integrated graft solutions. This can accelerate the translation of tissue engineering technologies into clinical use [184]. The development of innovative techniques for complex tissue systems aims to achieve biological cohesion between tissue engineering grafts and the host environment. Current efforts in this emerging field are focused on the formation of functional interfaces between different tissue types. Bionic scaffolds with pre-designed structural and functional gradients can facilitate interactions between various cells and biochemical signals, thereby promoting tissue interface regeneration [185]. These strategies focus on independently generating interfaces or as part of multi-organ composites, like bone-tendon grafts or bone-cartilage constructs [186]. The next section discusses strategies for combining cells and growth factors in gradient scaffolds to guide regeneration.

sec.title: Cell culture in gradient scaffolds
sec.p: Cells play a crucial role in the regeneration, composition, and homeostasis of graded tissue interfaces. Various stem cell types have shown promise in promoting interface healing. Three main approaches exist for interface tissue engineering: (1) co-culturing differentiated cells, such as myocytes, fibroblasts, chondrocytes, and osteoblasts, (2) monoculture of stem cell, and (3) co-culturing differentiated cells and stem cells [22]. For instance, Cooper et al. [187] seeded fibroblasts (FB) and osteoblasts (OB) at the two ends of degradable PLA scaffolds, investigated cell seeding and migration in a co-culture of specific scaffolds, and identified a co-culture medium formulation that allowed for low FB mineralization rate and OB mineralization. The results revealed consistently detectable tissue-specific DNA, glycosaminoglycans, and collagen on the scaffold, with no significant differences between different regions of the scaffold. Wang et al. [188] sequentially implanted heterogeneous cell populations: FB, chondrocytes (CC), and osteoblasts (OB) into pre-defined three functional zones (fibrous zone, cartilage-forming zone, and bone-forming zone) of a scaffold, creating a layered scaffold with three functional zones for fibre formation, cartilage formation, and bone formation. The study suggests that, through layered tissue integration, it is possible to replicate the in vitro transitional interface (fibre–fibre cartilage-bone) in decellularized tendons. Co-culturing differentiated cells can simulate cell populations found in natural interface tissues, but the limited regenerative capacity of mature fibroblasts, chondrocytes, and osteoblasts also restricts their application to some extent.

The most widely used stem cells are BMSC and adipose-derived stem cells (ADSCs), which can differentiate into various connective tissue lineages, including bone, cartilage, and tendons [189]. Compared to BMSCs, ADSCs can be easily isolated from the abundant autologous cells in adipose tissue, with relatively lower morbidity and pain at the donor site. Additionally, ADSCs exhibit higher in vitro proliferative capacity, lower senescence, and greater ability to generate tendon, bone, and cartilage-like tissues. Tatsuaki et al. [190] capitalized on the characteristics of ADSCs. During ACL reconstruction, they wrapped ADSC sheets around the centre of the graft. Results in a rabbit model indicated that ADSC sheets enhanced graft biomechanical strength, prevented bone tunnel enlargement, and promoted tendon-bone interface healing.

Furthermore, the presence of progenitor cells has been confirmed in T/L, often referred to as tendon-derived stem cells (TDSCs) or tendon stem/progenitor cells (TSPCs). Compared to BMSCs, TDSCs still possess tissue-specific differentiation potential, such as osteogenic, adipogenic, and chondrogenic differentiation, but they exhibit a greater tendency to form tenocytes than BMSCs [191]. In terms of the physiological niche, TDSCs as residents within the tendon, share the same microenvironment as tendon cells and are expected to play a crucial role in tissue maintenance and repair [192]. Co-culturing stem cells with differentiated cells can simulate the tendon-to-bone connection and promote stem cell differentiation. Research has shown that the interaction between fibroblasts and osteoblasts plays a crucial role in the differentiation of BMSC. This interaction facilitates paracrine factors and cellular physical contact [193]. Song et al. [194] conducted an in vitro study on the cell proliferation and matrix formation during co-culture of BMSCs and tendon cells (TCs). They observed higher cell proliferation rates in the co-culture group with additional mechanical stimulation and enhanced expression of type I, type III collagen, alkaline phosphatase, bone sialoprotein, and tendon-related proteins. Calejo et al. [195] established an in vitro co-culture model of human tendon-derived cells (hTDC) and pre-osteoblasts (pre-OBs). They found that in the presence of osteogenic medium (OM), the transcription levels of osteogenic (ALPL, RUNX2, SPP1) and interface-related genes (ACAN, COMP) were increased in the co-culture, and the expression of aggrecan was influenced by both OM and cell–cell interactions. Thus, in interface tissue regeneration strategies, integrating stem cells or co-culturing stem cells with differentiated cells on scaffolds presents a highly promising approach. A significant challenge in this area is exploring the cellular mechanisms involved in interface tissue development, as understanding these mechanisms can drive the regeneration of natural cell types [196]. Another challenge lies in comprehending how cells interact with the ECM [197]. This understanding is crucial for designing gradient scaffolds that allow for cell adhesion, growth, and interaction with biomaterials, thereby providing mechanical support and guiding tissue development.

sec.title: Growth factors in gradient scaffolds
sec.p:
Growth factors act as controllers of cell differentiation, proliferation, and matrix deposition and play a significant role in functional tissue development and repair. Growth factors are proteins secreted by cells and transmitted as signalling molecules to other cells through cell surface receptors. These molecules play a positive role in establishing complex structures at attachment sites, healing tissues after injury, and maintaining tissue homeostasis [198]. The choice of GF depends on the cell type used (multipotent or differentiated cells), the desired behaviour to induce (differentiation, proliferation, ECM deposition, or vascularization), and the type of scaffold (tendon/ligament, interface, or bone). As described in Table 4. In cases of acute injuries such as rotator cuff tears, the immediate response is vascular formation and inflammation at the injured interface. Chemoattractant such as vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), TGF-β, and platelet-derived growth factor (PDGF) are secreted locally, recruiting fibroblasts to the injury site. These fibroblasts recruit neutrophils, macrophages, and osteoclasts to the injury site to rebuild damaged tendons, fibrocartilage, and bones [42]. Growth factors like basic fibroblast growth factor (bFGF), growth differentiation factors (GDFs), platelet-derived growth factor (PDGF), and TGF-β3 are commonly used in tendon-ligament to bone tissue engineering [199]. Additionally, insulin-like growth factor (IGF), FGF, TGF-β, and PDGF are vital for the development and maintenance of collagen structures and high-level proteoglycans in cartilage and soft tissue [198].Table 4
Growth factors for bone-soft tissue interfacial tissue engineering.Table 4

25∼250 ng/mL[201]

VEGFBone regionPromoting vascular generation and increasing vascular permeabilityVEGF-binding peptide (BP): 0.1 mg/mL[202]

TGF-β1: 62.5 μg/mL[201]
TGF-β2: 100 ng/mL[203]
