bFGF: 400 μg/mL[130]
GDFTendon, cartilage regionInfluencing the proliferation and differentiation of soft tissue cells, GDF-5 upregulates tendon-related genes in TSPCsGDF5:100 ng/mL[203]
IGFCartilage regionIGF-1 increases ECM, type I and III collagen, elastinIGFBP-4 concentration: 1:1000 (IGFBP-4: dextran)[205]

Considering the biomimetic gradient characteristics of scaffolds in interface tissue engineering, the combination of various GFs and the formation of a gradient will be necessary to provide seed cell populations with different proliferation and differentiation stimuli [22]. Research indicates that the delivery of a single growth factor may not be sufficient to reconstruct a multiphase structure of the tendon-bone interface. For example, the delivery of PDGF-BB and BMP-2 may provide a synergistic effect to generate a multiphase structure resembling the T/L-bone tissue [206]. Therefore, multiple GFs should be released from the scaffold in a spatiotemporal manner gradually and sequentially. Dorcemus et al. [201] formed OC grafts with spatial growth factor loading and distribution by adsorptive loading BMP-2 onto PLGA scaffolds while doping TGF-β1 into the hydrogel phase. The dual growth factor profile proved to be beneficial for cartilage formation in the upper cartilage region of the scaffold, while also supporting osteogenesis in the lower bone region of the scaffold. Furthermore, in order to achieve controllable release of growth factors, Sonia et al. [203] through heparin functionalization, coupled TGF-β2 and GDF5 with silk protein scaffolds. TGF-β2 and GDF5 maintained biological activity at low doses and achieved controlled sustained release. This co-delivery of GFs also enhanced the expression of cartilage markers and type II collagen on an isotropic porous matrix. In a recent study, Bai et al. [130] combined various GFs and used 3D printing to construct a bio-scaffold with layer-specific, growth factor-loaded microspheres (μS), including bFGF, TGF-β, and BMP-2. This induced the in-situ region-specific differentiation of embedded human umbilical cord mesenchymal stem cells (hUCMSCs) towards tendon, cartilage, and bone tissues, resulting in the functional reconstruction of the tendon-to-bone interface in vivo to achieve functional regeneration of attachment point tissues. These studies indicate that the release of co growth factors in gradient scaffolds has a positive effect on the regeneration of soft and hard tissue interfaces.

Platelet-rich plasma (PRP), enriched with various GFs, serves as an abundant reservoir for multifactorial therapy, overcoming the limitations of single-factor treatments, such as those focused solely on inducing interface healing biologically [207]. PRP may promote the regeneration of degenerative tendons by stimulating scar tissue differentiation, thereby improving rotator cuff healing. This is mainly attributed to the fact that PRP therapy can be used to fill or cover defects, locally releasing the necessary cell GFs for wound healing and stimulating cellular activity [208]. Although PRP preparations with different platelet concentrations are used in experiments and clinical applications, there is no clear optimum platelet concentration, and the relationship between concentration and effectiveness remains unclear [209].

sec.title: Emerging trends and strategies
sec.p: As our understanding of the biology and biomechanics of interface tissues advances, along with developments in tissue engineering, researchers can now apply novel therapies to enhance interface tissue healing. Various engineering approaches and scaffold designs have been employed to achieve the intricate regeneration of interface tissues, aiming to replicate the graded structure, mechanical properties, and composition between bone and soft tissues [20]. Recently, a promising fabrication technique derived from 3D printing, known as four-dimensional (4D) printing, has emerged. This technology enables the creation of smart materials capable of changing shape over time in response to stimuli, allowing static 3D-printed structures to move into precise positions and shapes autonomously [210]. Specifically, 4D printing employs programmable materials, typically shape-memory polymers, thermo-responsive materials, or hydrogels, which are pre-designed to undergo reversible deformation in response to external stimuli. For instance, these materials can transition from one form to another when exposed to temperature changes [211]. In biomedical engineering, particularly in soft-hard interface tissue engineering, 4D printing offers excellent customization capabilities, facilitating easier implantation and on-demand actuation in response to specific stimuli [212]. For instance, cell-laden gradient hydrogels made from biopolymers and microgels prepared through 4D printing can undergo programmable shape changes, promoting the formation of bone-like and cartilage-like tissues [213]. Additionally, melt electrospinning writing (MEW) has emerged as an alternative additive manufacturing process. MEW enables the production of fibrous scaffolds with precise macro- and microstructural characteristics, such as porosity (i.e., pore size), fibre diameter, and alignment direction [214]. This precise control over structural features opens new avenues for the creation of biomimetic interface tissues with specific characteristics [215]. By combining MEW with inkjet bioprinting to fabricate sequential bio-scaffolds [216], or integrating MEW with electrospinning to prepare composite membranes with gradient pore structures [217], these emerging fabrication techniques hold great potential for interface tissue engineering.

Although providing biochemical and biophysical cues of natural ECM in gradient scaffolds is crucial for the delivery and recruitment of stem cells and matrix cells [218,219], the mismatch in mechanical properties between tissues and biomaterials remains a significant challenge in soft-hard tissue regeneration engineering [220]. The use of various innovative technologies has introduced new approaches for the design and fabrication of gradient scaffolds, enabling precise spatial and temporal modifications of scaffold properties. However, T/L regeneration is a complex process that requires dynamic mechanical properties and sufficient space to regulate collagen remodelling [221]. Emerging research focuses on designing regenerative scaffolds with gradient degradation and spatial adaptability by modulating the degradation rates of degradable fibres. Such scaffolds hold great potential as viable alternatives for T/L reconstruction [222,223]. Adjusting the material degradation rate is crucial to ensure that the scaffold provides adequate support during the initial healing phase while allowing for natural tissue integration as the scaffold gradually degrades over time [224].

The scaffold degradation process not only affects its mechanical support but also plays a crucial role in regulating the immune response [225]. This ensures that the scaffold promotes natural healing rather than triggering adverse reactions. Increasing evidence suggests that the immune system, particularly macrophages, plays a critical role in regeneration across various tissues. Macrophage can be classified into M1 pro-inflammatory and M2 anti-inflammatory phenotypes based on their functions and surface markers. An imbalance between M1 and M2 macrophages results in incomplete regeneration, and modulating macrophage phenotypes through chemical, physical, or biological therapies can facilitate tissue regeneration [226]. The stiffness gradient of polymer substrates has been demonstrated to modulate macrophage differentiation via the NF-κB p65 pathway. Polyacrylamide hydrogels with low stiffness can enhance the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), and oxidative stress is associated with the activation of the NF-κB p65 pathway [227]. Therefore, another emerging trend in using gradient scaffolds for regenerating soft-hard tissue interfaces is the design of microenvironmental cues that actively trigger immune responses [228,229]. This can be achieved by regulating inflammation through exosomes derived from exogenous or endogenous MSCs, which in turn accelerates neovascularization and modulates both osteogenesis and osteoclast genesis [230].

sec.title: Conclusion and prospects
sec.p: With advancing technologies, scaffolds that integrate multiple cues can effectively simulate the microenvironment of the native attachment site. Carefully designed bio-scaffolds have been shown to enhance interface healing by inducing oriented collagen fibres and new bone formation. Considerable progress has been made in the structural design and material composition for bone-soft tissue interface repair. Bio-scaffolds with gradient features in composition, structure, and function have proven to be a promising tool for interface tissue engineering.

The ongoing development of technologies such as electrospinning and 3D printing facilitates the creation of gradient scaffolds that integrate various cues to closely simulate the ECM. Most scaffolds mimic natural attachment points at the microscale, but accurately replicating the functional attachment points at the nanoscale remains challenging. This is due to the gradient transitions and multi-tissue structures present within the small dimensions of the soft-hard tissue interface. Therefore, a key future challenge is the construction of nanoscale biomimetic gradient scaffolds within these small areas. In the future, more advanced manufacturing techniques such as 4D printing, microfluidic control, and multi-component electrospinning, could be developed to achieve more refined gradient structures and functionalities.

Integrating multiple functions into gradient scaffolds, including promoting cell differentiation, regulating growth factor release, and providing necessary mechanical support, remains a complex challenge. Future research could explore smart materials and controlled release systems that combine biodegradability, drug delivery, and signal transduction, enabling multifunctional integration and dynamic regulation within gradient scaffolds, thereby enhancing tissue repair and regeneration at the interface. Additionally, gradient scaffolds for bone-soft tissue repair must withstand high mechanical loads. However, ensuring their long-term mechanical stability and sustained functionality remains a significant challenge. Future efforts should focus on investigating the long-term mechanical properties of scaffold materials and optimising scaffold design to improve durability under biomechanical stress.

To date, research has shown that each type of gradient cue offers distinct advantages. However, combining multiple factors may be the most effective strategy for tissue interface repair. The integration of biochemical cues and mechanical stimulation has already shown promise in tendon tissue engineering [231]. Scaffold-bioreactor systems, which provide appropriate stimuli, have been shown to more effectively guide tissue differentiation for interface repair.

Furthermore, maintaining the phenotypes of different cell types within the heterogeneous environment of gradient scaffolds remains challenging, particularly during long-term culture and tissue regeneration. Different cell types have distinct biochemical and physical microenvironmental requirements, and current scaffolds often struggle to meet these complex demands simultaneously. Therefore, exploring the optimal combination of biochemical and physical cues, along with advanced cell culture strategies, is crucial. Incorporating the controlled release of bioactive factors, such as growth factors and cytokines, within the same scaffold ensures the precise targeting of specific cells in the appropriate spatial and temporal context [232]. This approach helps regulate cell behaviour precisely, thereby promoting region-specific tissue regeneration. Additionally, repairing the interface between bone and soft tissue often involves using different materials or cells, which may trigger immune reactions and rejection issues. Therefore, further research is required to optimize material biocompatibility and minimize immune reactions. Future studies should focus on the immunomodulation of gradient scaffolds in biomaterials to improve their efficacy.

Although biomimetic components within scaffolds demonstrate excellent performance, current research predominantly focuses on outcomes in animal models and often lacks in-depth insights into stem cell tracking, signalling pathways, and cell–cell interactions. Many studies primarily focus on short-term effects, with limited evaluation of long-term outcomes and potential complications following repair. Long-term follow-up studies are essential for understanding the durability and safety of repairs. Future research should continue to explore how biomimetic gradients interact with tissue regeneration. Further attention should be given to the interactions between biomechanics and biochemistry to improve understanding of the mechanisms governing cell behaviour and tissue repair. This understanding is essential for developing biomimetic scaffolds with more advanced gradient characteristics.

In summary, the development of multifunctional gradient scaffolds will expand their application in repairing bone-soft tissue interface injuries, as scaffold designs continue to be optimised to meet the biochemical and mechanical requirements of diverse tissue cell types. Future research will focus on improving scaffold resolution, regulating degradation rates, and enhancing mechanical properties to promote tissue integration. Moreover, the development of immunomodulatory materials could provide more effective solutions, potentially advancing the use of gradient scaffolds in personalised and precision medicine.

sec.title: Conflict of interest
sec.p: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
